answer:
The shared procedural rule is the qualification and disqualification of witnesses in civil disputes (sākṣi-prakaraṇam / witness law).

**Yājñavalkya Smṛti, Chapter 2 (Vyavahārādhyāya), Yj_2.68–2.72** — tight, compressed sutra-style:

• Yj_2.68 (p-5c0bb3): Positive qualifications — ascetics, charitable men, men of good family, truth-tellers, those for whom dharma is paramount, upright men, those with sons, and those with wealth.
• Yj_2.69 (p-18320c): At least three witnesses, devoted to the rites of revelation and tradition; same caste and varṇa as the parties — or anyone may witness for anyone.
• Yj_2.70 (p-478abd): Disqualified — 'Women, children, the aged, gamblers, the intoxicated, the insane, those accused of a great sin, actors, heretics, forgers, and those with defective sense organs are not competent as witnesses.'
• Yj_2.71 (p-f5049f): Further disqualified — 'Outcastes, friends, those with a pecuniary interest, associates, enemies, thieves, perpetrators of violence, those convicted of a crime, and the banished.'
• Yj_2.72 (p-33a082): Exception — even one witness approved by both parties is acceptable; anyone can testify in cases of adultery, theft, assault, and violence.

Five verses. The rule is announced, affirmatively stated, negatively delimited, and closed with an exception — all with zero rhetorical padding.

**Manusmṛti, Chapter 8 (Chapter on Legal Procedure), Mn_8.61–8.72** — expansive verse form (all under corpus paragraph p-803bc3):

• Mn_8.61: Manu introduces the topic discursively — 'What kind of witnesses should be appointed by creditors in legal proceedings, such I will now declare, and how they should speak the truth.'
• Mn_8.62: 'Householders with sons, of established lineage, born of the Kshatriya, Vaishya, and Shudra varnas, when called by the plaintiff, are fit to give evidence, but not just anyone, except in a time of distress.'
• Mn_8.63: Trustworthy men from all varnas who know all the Dharmas and are not greedy.
• Mn_8.64: Exclusions — connected by wealth, close relatives, helpers, enemies, those with a proven fault, afflicted by disease, defamed.
• Mn_8.65: More exclusions — king, artisan, actor, śrotriya, religious-order wearer, one who has renounced.
• Mn_8.66: More exclusions — overly dependent, robber, those doing forbidden acts, old man, child, one alone, lowest caste, impaired senses.
• Mn_8.67: More exclusions — distressed, intoxicated, insane, hungry, thirsty, weary, lustful, angry, thief.
• Mn_8.68: Segregated procedure — women for women, twice-born for twice-born, Shudras for Shudras.
• Mn_8.69: Exception — anyone who witnessed something inside a house, in a forest, or in a matter of life and death may testify.
• Mn_8.70–8.72: Further nuances — emergency fallback witnesses, warning about children/sick, and the rule that in violence/theft/adultery cases witnesses should not be examined too strictly.

Twelve verses covering the same terrain as Yājñavalkya's five.

**Comparison**: Both texts share the identical legal logic — a positive definition of the ideal witness (householder, truthful, knowledgeable, not greedy), a tiered list of the disqualified (intoxicated, insane, interested party, enemy, actor, criminal, impaired), and an emergency exception for violent or secret crimes. Yājñavalkya's sutra-style compresses this into lean binary pairs: qualified vs. disqualified, general vs. exception. Manu re-expands each category into its own verse, adds procedural narrative ('I will now declare'), segregates by varna-of-the-parties (Mn_8.68), and multiplies sub-exceptions (Mn_8.70). The underlying rule is identical; the rhetorical shape is utterly different — Yājñavalkya gives a legislator's code, Manu gives a teacher's lecture.
